texts.label: text
texts.orig: Disposal of saline ef fl uent from desalination plants, agricultural drainage water, and other industries is an increasing problem worldwide. Saline ef fl uent has long been considered waste brine and traditional approaches to its disposal have included evaporation ponds, deep wells, and coastal discharge. However, such ef fl uent is now being considered as a saline resource; therefore, alternative approaches have been developed to extract available salts and to recover puri fi ed water. In this study, techniques to dispose of rejected brine were investigated and alternative salt recovery methods that have been developed and widely employed were reviewed. Evaporation and cooling to extract salts from retentate has been widely used worldwide, but use of the membrane separation techniquehasbeenincreasingrapidlyowingtothedevelopmentofcheaperandhigherperformancemembranes. Electrodialysis, ion-exchange, eutectic freezing, and chemical processing can also be used to recover salts from retentate. Currently, hybrid systems that combine two or more techniques, such as the nano fi ltration (pretreatment) -reverse osmosis (concentration) -thermal processes (crystallization), are being developed and used actively to increase the amount of extracted salt and reduce the fi nal volume of rejected brine. Economic analysis of concentrate utilization in comparison with disposal was performed in this study. Based on the cost of producing salts, the multi-stage fl ash (MSF) distillation and electrodialysis and Dow chemical process were relatively expensive when compared to nano fi ltration and membrane crystallization, evaporation, and ionexchange techniques. However, technical developments for increasing the performance ef fi ciency must be further investigated to reduce the cost of desalting processes.

texts.orig: 1. Introduction
texts.text: 1. Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Managing the high salinity concentrate that is generated during membrane water desalination is a primary issue in desalination of inland brackish water. Concentrates from desalting techniques contain inorganic salts and other compounds that may be puri fi ed for commercial value. Theoverallgoalofthisstudywastoreviewcurrentlyemployedprocesses. Speci fi cally, this study investigated the existing residual brine disposal techniques, reviewed the currently available technical literature with respect to concentrate management, and explored possible salt recovery techniques for commercial marketing and utilization.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Previous studies have shown that salts in brine can be recovered and puri fi ed as commercial products. Salts have been recovered from salty lakes [1 -3], sea water [4 -7], textile ef fl uent [8], concentrate at a sugar decolorization plant [9], and industrial ef fl uent [10 -12]. Evaporation and cooling (sodium chloride, sodium sulfate, potassium salt, and magnesium salt), adding sodium carbonate (magnesium sulfate), nano fi ltration (sodium chloride), membrane crystallization (sodium chloride, and magnesium sulfate), calcinations (sodium sulfate), eutectic freezing (magnesium sulfate), and electrolytic processes (chlorine, and magnesium chloride) have been used for the recovery of salt. Additionally, Jenkins et al. (1998) [13] investigated the possible vitri fi cation of salt and its use as a source of raw material for glass making and other industrial markets.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3. Salt recovery techniques
texts.text: 3. Salt recovery techniques

texts.label: text
texts.orig: There are several basic techniques that can remove dissolved solids from water; distillation (evaporation and cooling), membrane separation, electrodialysis, ion exchange, eutectic freezing, and chemical processes (e.g. calcinations). Membrane separation and electrodialysis use membranes to remove minerals and dissolved solids from water. Distillation separates pure water from brackish or seawater via evaporation and condensation. Ion exchange involves the exchange of dissolved mineral ions in the water for more desirable ions by passing the water through cation and anion exchange columns. Eutectic freezing crystallization separates inorganic aqueous solutions into pure ice and solidi fi ed solutes by freezing at the eutectic point. Chemical reactions can be used by adding other chemical solutions to retentate, while calcinations can be used for the recovery of target salts. Several factors have in fl uenced the selection of suitable salt recovery technologies, such as the composition of the feed water, target recovered salts, and other site-speci fi c factors, such as weather and transportation.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3.1. Evaporation and cooling
texts.text: 3.1. Evaporation and cooling

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Salt and mineral separation from brine, seawater, or brackish water can be accomplished by distillation, in which water is vaporized from the salt solution and subsequently recovered by condensation.
texts.text: Salt and mineral separation from brine, seawater, or brackish water can be accomplished by distillation, in which water is vaporized from the salt solution and subsequently recovered by condensation.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The various distillation processes are used to distill water and separate salt and minerals on a commercial or semi-commercial scale including multi-stage fl ash (MSF), multiple-effect evaporation (MEE), and vapor compression (VC). These processes operate on the principle of reducing the vapor pressure of water within the unit to permit boiling to occur at low temperatures without adding heat. In the MSF distillation, incoming feed water passing through the heating stages is heated more in the heat recovery sections of each subsequent stage. Then the water is further heated using externally supplied steam to increase the water to its highest temperature, after which the water is passed through the various stages where fl ashing occurs. The vapor pressure of each stage is controlled so that the water at the proper temperature and successively lower vapor pressure enters each stage to cause vaporization. The fresh water is produced by condensation of the water vapor and collected at each stage. In the MEE process, incoming feed water is heated and then passed through a series of evaporators. The produced steam is condensed on one side of a tube wall while the feed water is evaporated on the other side. The heat produced by condensing the steam was used for evaporating the feed water. Vapor compression (VC) distillation is another desalination technique, in which the heated inlet feed water passes to a brine heater, where it is heated with vapor that has been discharged by the evaporator compressor. The heated feed then passes into the evaporator, after which the generated vapor is compressed and used as the steam supply for the brine heater and the condensate is discharged as the product stream. The compressor can be connected to a fl ash chamber or coupled to a MEE system. In a simple solar distillation process, saline feed water is supplied continuously or intermittently to a basin of water inside a glass-covered enclosure in which water vapor rising from sun-heated brine condenses on a cooler inside the surface of the glass. The droplets of distilled water that run down the glass are then collected in troughs along the lower edges of the glass, after which the distilled water is channeled to storage tanks.

texts.orig: In a previous study, salts were recovered from salty lake water using multiple evaporation and cooling methods [3]. Speci fi cally, sodium chloride was recovered from brine by evaporating until a speci fi c gravity of 1.240 was attained, after which thenardite (Na2SO4) and bloedite (Na2 SO4·MgSO4·4H2O) were produced by cooling to -10 °C. The carnalite (KMgCl3·6H2O), halite (NaCl), and langbeinite (2MgSO4·K2SO4) were then recovered by evaporation, and at the last step, bischo fi te (MgCl2·6H2O) and kieserite (MgSO4·H2O) were obtained. This technique has also been applied to recover sodium sulfate decahydrate and sodium chloride from lake brine and seawater [1,2,5,21,22]. In addition, a refrigerator-heat-pump was used to recover fresh water and salts from seawater [23]. In this study, a salt water chamber was continuously re fi lled with seawater at atmospheric pressure. The seawater was then evaporated into a vacuum chamber, after which the water vapor was condensed on top of a fresh water chamber. The salt was then precipitated at the bottom of the evaporator chamber and removed by mechanical methods [23]. The evaporation technique was used as a fi nal stage for the recovery of salts and disposal of the brine produced from different desalting processes [23 -30]. texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3.2. Membrane separation
texts.text: 3.2. Membrane separation

texts.label: text
texts.orig: If a solution such as salt water is placed on one side of a semipermeable membrane and water is placed on the other side, then there is a natural tendency for water to diffuse through the membrane to the solute side until an equilibrium osmotic pressure is achieved (osmosis). In a process known as reverse osmosis (RO), pressure substantially greater than the osmotic pressure is applied to the solute side, then water diffuses from the solution through the membrane to the fresh water side. This process is continuous and separates the feed fl uid into two streams without the need to regenerate the membrane. Speci fi cally, a concentrate stream containing the impurities and a high purity water stream known as the permeate are produced.
texts.text: If a solution such as salt water is placed on one side of a semipermeable membrane and water is placed on the other side, then there is a natural tendency for water to diffuse through the membrane to the solute side until an equilibrium osmotic pressure is achieved (osmosis). In a process known as reverse osmosis (RO), pressure substantially greater than the osmotic pressure is applied to the solute side, then water diffuses from the solution through the membrane to the fresh water side. This process is continuous and separates the feed fl uid into two streams without the need to regenerate the membrane. Speci fi cally, a concentrate stream containing the impurities and a high purity water stream known as the permeate are produced.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: RO is a high-pressure, energy-ef fi cient technique for dewatering process streams, concentrating low-molecular-weight substances in solution, or purifying wastewater. RO has the ability to concentrate all dissolved and suspended solids, while producing a permeate that contains a very low concentration of dissolved solids.
texts.text: RO is a high-pressure, energy-ef fi cient technique for dewatering process streams, concentrating low-molecular-weight substances in solution, or purifying wastewater. RO has the ability to concentrate all dissolved and suspended solids, while producing a permeate that contains a very low concentration of dissolved solids.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The nanofi ltration (NF) technique is primarily used for the removal of bivalent and monovalent ions such as heavy metals. NF essentially uses a coarse form of a RO membrane. Because NF uses less fi ne membranes, the feed pressure of the NF system is generally lower than that of RO systems. In addition, NF has a lower fouling rate than RO. NF is a special process selected when RO and Ultra fi ltration (UF) are not the ideal choice for separation. NF can perform separation applications that are not otherwise economically feasible, such as demineralization, color removal, and desalination. During the concentration of organic solutes, suspended solids, and polyvalent ions, the permeate contains monovalent ions and low-molecular-weight organic solutions such as alcohol.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: This method not only removes organic substances, but also inorganic content from wastewater. Based on the disposal of heavily loaded industrial wastewater, recovery of substances from wastewater, reduction in the load on treatment plants and the wastewater quantity produced, RO is a very economical and safe process that has already been shown to be successful for practical use [32]. Membranes employed in RO are usually made of cellulose acetate, aromatic polyamide, polyimide, polysulfones, or thin fi lm composites. Membranes can be designed to remove speci fi c inorganic and organic contaminants, such as trihalomethanes. The ef fi ciency of RO operations will increase and costs will decrease as membranes are improved. Such improvements may include increased rejection of salt, increased membrane resistance to compaction, chlorine, and microorganisms, and large-scale production of standardized RO elements. There are many commercial RO membranes available, including cellulose acetate and polyamide membranes, which are suitable for water treatment on a technical scale. These membranes have salt rejection rates of cellulose acetate and polyamide of 94 -97% and 94%, respectively [32]. There are also a wide variety of membrane systems available for the treatment of wastewater. Pretreatment processes are essential to all systems to ensure a long service life of membranes and trouble-free operation. Adequate pretreatment is critical for the successful use of RO membranes for desalination.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Water separation processes were demonstrated in the 1960s, and combined processes were then proposed in which RO units and crystallizers were used to recover salts from the solutions [33]. Karelin et al. (1996) [34] investigated the capacities of NF and RO highpressure membrane systems to separate a high-concentration solution. The RO membranes were able to retain NaCl, even if its concentration in aqueous solutions was near the saturation level. Membrane separation techniques have also been applied for the reuse of salts and water from dyeing ef fl uent [35,36], water recovery from brackish water [37,38], separation of salt from wastewater produced by chemical industries [39,40], and treatment of agricultural drainage water (ADW) for non-agricultural purposes [41]. To recover fresh water from ADW, an aerated lagoon and a two stage RO system was used and the retentate was sent to an evaporation pond for disposal [41]. Membrane technology was also used to recover sodium chloride from waste brine at a sugar decolorization plant [9]. RO coupled with NF was found to be more economical than a multiple effect evaporator for salt recovery from textile ef fl uents [42].

texts.orig: Economically, RO is relatively inexpensive to install, operate, and maintain, and it consumes less energy than other desalination methods. However, membrane separation always produces a relatively large volume of concentrate that must be disposed off or receive further processing.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Reverse osmosis is generally not selective for a speci fi c target compound. Accordingly, other constituents present may also need to be separated. As a result, RO is not-cost effective as an add-on process for a speci fi c target compound after other compounds have been eliminated during pretreatment. Determination of the correct materials is the most important factor when developing RO desalination plant equipment to ensure that a long operation life is attained.
texts.text: Reverse osmosis is generally not selective for a speci fi c target compound. Accordingly, other constituents present may also need to be separated. As a result, RO is not-cost effective as an add-on process for a speci fi c target compound after other compounds have been eliminated during pretreatment. Determination of the correct materials is the most important factor when developing RO desalination plant equipment to ensure that a long operation life is attained.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3.3. Electrodialysis (ED)
texts.text: 3.3. Electrodialysis (ED)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Electrodialysis consists of a large number of cells alternately contained within anion-exchange and cation-exchange membranes and arranged between an anode and a cathode. Under the in fl uence of the applied electric fi eld, the ions migrate towards the electrodes. Because alternate membranes are permeable only to cations or anions, the water cells between the membranes are alternately depleted and enriched with salt ions. The cation membranes allow only positively charged ions to diffuse through them.
texts.text: Electrodialysis consists of a large number of cells alternately contained within anion-exchange and cation-exchange membranes and arranged between an anode and a cathode. Under the in fl uence of the applied electric fi eld, the ions migrate towards the electrodes. Because alternate membranes are permeable only to cations or anions, the water cells between the membranes are alternately depleted and enriched with salt ions. The cation membranes allow only positively charged ions to diffuse through them.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Electrodialysis has been used to desalt surface and ground water since the 1950s [43]. This technique has been investigated for its feasibility and performance in the desalination of brackish water [44 -50], ef fl uent treatment and recycling of industrial process streams [51 -53], salt production [29], [54 -57], and recovery of organic acids from waste salt solutions [58]. Electrodialysis can be used as a pre-treatment or a preconcentration method before the brine is supplied to an evaporative crystallizer [29,56,57]. This method can reduce the concentration of calcium or sulfate ions to avoid gypsum crystallization during further evaporation [29]. During the process, fouling by colloidal material, organics, and biogrowth should be considered and treated to help sustain the equipment. These problems must be overcome for electrodialysis to be used more widely.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this process, feed water is passed through a column containing the active form of a solid cation exchanger, which is an organic resin that contains hydrogen ions and is capable of exchanging them against the positive ions contained in the feed water.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A continuous EFC system based on direct cooling in a mixed crystallizer followed by salt -ice separation has been developed at the bench scale [67]. Speci fi cally, EFC was used to recover magnesium sulfate heptahydrate (MgSO4·7H2O) from a magnesium sulfate industrial stream emitted from fl ue gas desulphurization [12]. MgSO4·12H2O was formed in the crystallizer and recrystallization into MgSO4·7H2O occurred spontaneously during the subsequent fi ltration and drying above 0.4 °C. The produced salt crystals were reasonably pure. Additionally, a cooled disk column crystallizer (CDCC) that employed the EFC technique was developed to recover CuSO4 crystals from copper sulfate solution [68,69]. Crystallization was achieved by indirect cooling with wiped disks and separation by means of gravity. In this system, the liquid feed stream enters the column at the center, while ice leaves the column at the top and salt is discharged from the bottom. When compared to conventional evaporative crystallization, the energy required for EFC can be reduced by up to 70% and the 100% conversion into water and salt is an advantage [68]. EFC system analysis and modeling have been conducted to simulate its transient behavior [70 -72].

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3.6. Chemical processes
texts.text: 3.6. Chemical processes

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Generally, retentate contains calcium, magnesium, sodium, sulfate, chloride, and bicarbonate. Gypsum (CaSO4) precipitation occurs fi rst as the concentration of the retentate increases due to its solubility characteristics (0.2 g of gypsum is dissolved in 100 g of water, which results in minor deviations in temperature). To prevent the loss of SO4 2 -via gypsum precipitation and to recover more Na2SO4 and MgSO4, the precipitation of gypsum can be limited by adding NaHCO3/Na2CO3 so that Ca 2+ ions are precipitated as carbonates. Calcination is a thermal treatment process applied to ores and other solid materials to induce thermal decomposition, phase transition, or removal of a volatile fraction. The calcination process normally occurs at temperatures below the melting point of the product materials. The process of calcination derives its name from its most common application, the decomposition of calcium carbonate (limestone) to calcium oxide (lime). The product of calcination is usually referred to as calcine, regardless of the actual minerals undergoing thermal treatment. Calcination is conducted in furnaces or reactors (sometimes referred to as kilns) of various designs, including shaft furnaces, rotary kilns, multiple hearth furnaces, and fl uidized bed reactors.

texts.orig: Recovery of CaCO3 from nanofi ltration retentate has been conducted using reactive precipitation by adding NaHCO3/Na2CO3 aqueous solutions to the retentate [7]. Speci fi cally, to recover MgSO4·7H2O from the retentate produced by seawater nano fi ltration, Ca 2+ ions were precipitated as carbonates by reaction with NaHCO3/ Na2CO3 solutions so that calcium sulfate precipitation, which causes the reduction of SO4 2 -content in the solution following limitation of the recovery of magnesium sulfate, was limited. Sodium (bi) carbonate solutions were produced by reactive transfer of carbon dioxide into sodium hydroxide solutions. This technique has been used to recover magnesium sulfate from high sulfate brine, rockforming minerals, and salty lake water [1,2,4].

In the dye industry, wastewater produced from dyeing processes contains a large amount of Na2SO4.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3.7. Combination of techniques
texts.text: 3.7. Combination of techniques

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Several techniques have been combined to increase system performance ef fi ciency and reduce fi nal rejected brine, or even achieve zero-discharge. The combined techniques were ultra fi ltration as a pretreatment, nano fi ltration for concentrating brine, multi-stage fl ash for desalting water, and crystallization for recovering salts [27]. Alternatively, electrodialysis for concentrating brine, multi-stage fl ash for desalting water, and crystallization for recovering salts were employed [29,56], or the same process with RO instead of the multistage fl ash [25]. Discharged brine from RO was supplied to an ionexchange membrane electrodialyzer, and the concentrated brine from the electrodialyzer was then sent to a multi-effect vacuum evaporator to crystallize the salts [57]. Sequential extraction of a variety of salts from rejected brine with high levels of dissolved sulfate, potassium, and magnesium salts has also been conducted using multiple evaporation and cooling of saline wastewater, chemical reactions, crystallization, washing, and dewatering [15,28]. The combined process was also applied to recover salts and water from dye house ef fl uents [36]. Prefi ltration has been employed to remove cotton fi bers and other large size components from dye ef fl uents, after which neutralization, and nano fi ltration was applied to separate the mineral salts and the hydrolyzed dye and dyeing auxiliaries. RO can be conducted as a fi nal step, if necessary, to increase the concentration of mineral salts and produce water that can be reused for the dyeing process.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Economic analysis of concentrate utilization in comparison with disposal was performed. Speci fi cally, a literature review was conducted to evaluate salt recovery techniques, such as MSF and electrodialysis [24], nanofi ltration and membrane crystallization [73], the Dow chemical process [74], VC and electrodialysis [25], and RO and ion-exchange [75]. These techniques have previously been analyzed and compared with each other and with brine disposal by deep well injection and evaporation basin methods in the US [76]. Simpli fi ed fl ow sheet for each treatment processes for cost comparison is given in Fig. 1, and feed analysis to each process in Table 1. The levelized cash fl ow approach was used for this analysis.

texts.orig: 5. Conclusions
texts.text: 5. Conclusions

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Here, techniques for salt recovery from retentate were reviewed. Literature survey revealed that evaporation and cooling are the most common methods used for salt recovery, but that membrane separation techniques have gained popularity worldwide as cheaper membranes with better performance have developed. Ion-exchange, electrodialysis, eutectic freezing, and chemical reaction are also increasingly being used. For better system performance, hybrid systems that employ combined nano fi ltration, reverse osmosis, and thermal processes have been developed and applied. To select suitable techniques for salt recovery, several parameters, such as geological, hydrological, climatic, and economic conditions, should be considered for each site in advance.
